message:
perf(ast_matcher): cancel worker on parent timeout instead of leaking

`run_ast_match_with_timeout` spawned a worker thread to run
`find_matches_ast` and used `mpsc::sync_channel` plus
`rx.recv_timeout(timeout)` to bound the parent's wait. On
`RecvTimeoutError::Timeout` the parent returned a timeout error to the
caller — but did not signal the worker, so the worker happily kept
parsing/iterating until ITS internal `check_ast_timeout` poll caught
up. Under burst hook traffic that piles up: a stream of 200 ms hooks
can leak a stack of N tree-sitter parsers, each running for the full
deadline before noticing.

Threads cannot be force-killed safely in Rust (the worker may hold a
tree-sitter parser mid-iteration; aborting would corrupt the parser
arena), so this commit adds a cooperative cancellation flag instead:

- Allocate `Arc<AtomicBool>` cancel flag at spawn time; clone for the
  worker.
- `check_ast_timeout` now takes `&AtomicBool` and short-circuits on
  `cancel.load(Ordering::Relaxed)` in addition to the wall-clock
  comparison. The check is called between each pattern and between
  every iteration of `root.find_all`, so the worker bails on its
  next yield point — typically within one node match.
- Both `RecvTimeoutError::Timeout` and `RecvTimeoutError::Disconnected`
  now `cancel.store(true, Relaxed)` before returning. We deliberately
  do NOT `join` the handle: joining would block the hook past its
  200 ms hard ceiling if the worker is mid-parse. The cancellation
  flag bounds the worker's own wall-clock so a leaked handle still
  terminates promptly.

Net effect: parent timeout latency is unchanged (still bounded by
`recv_timeout`), but worker thread lifetime drops from
"full parse + match" to "until next `check_ast_timeout` poll" — at
most a handful of ms in practice. Sustained-burst leak goes away.
